coredata.dc:title: A hybrid ratiometric probe for glucose detection based on synchronous responses to fluorescence quenching and resonance light scattering enhancement of boronic acid functionalized carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zou, Wen-Sheng
Ye, Cui-Hong
Wang, Ya-Qin
Li, Wei-Hua
Huang, Xian-Huai
coredata.dc:description: Enzymatic assay for glucose as a traditional protocol has always been suffering from cost-expensiveness and time-consumption, and instability and denaturation of glucose oxidase. Boronate affinity chemosensors currently became the research hotspots in the field of sugar sensing. Herein a simple but effective hybrid ratiometric platform has been addressed for glucose detection based on synchronous fluorescence quenching and resonance light scattering (RLS) enhancement of boronic acid functionalized carbon dots (C-dots). The C-dots was fabricated by one step “synthesis-modification integration” strategy using 3-aminophenyl boronic acid and sodium citrate as precursors. The retained boronic acid groups on the C-dots surface can assembly with cis-diols group of glucose to form coordination compound, which resulted in the fluorescence quenching of C-dots by aggregation states. Also, the aggregation among C-dots simultaneously induced the great RLS enhancement of C-dots because of the existence of two pairs of cis-diols groups. A hybrid ratiometric chemosensor for glucose was therefore constructed. Significantly, due to the inert surface of the C-dots, this ratiometric probe can assay down to 10 μM glucose without the interference from a variety of biomolecules and metal ions in real water samples, and exhibits an excellent selectivity over other analogues. The present sensing system has been successfully applied to the assay of glucose in urine samples. It displays great promise as a practical platform for glucose detection due to its simplicity and effectiveness.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hybrid ratiometric probe
Carbon dots (C-dots)
Glucose detection
Resonance light scattering (RLS)
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of boronic acid functionalized C-dots
Collection of real urine samples and procedures for glucose sensing
Synchronous responses of fluorescence quenching and RLS enhancement of C-dots for glucose
Selectivity of the boronic acid functionalized C-dots chemosensor for glucose
Boron affinity interaction between C-dots and glucose, and influence factors
Synchronous RS enhancement but fluorescence quenching-based hybrid ratiometric probe
Application of C-dots for ratiometric assay of glucose in urine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: One-step “synthesis-modification integration” strategy for fabrication of boronic acid functionalized C-dots (A); Working principle for glucose sensing (B). Carboxylic acid and hydroxyl groups on surface of C-dots are omitted for clarity in B.
FT-IR spectra of the C-dots and 3-APBA (A). XPS scanning spectrum of the C-dots (B). XPS high resolution survey scan of C 1 s (C) and B 1 s (D) of the C-dots.

Normalized UV-vis, excitation and emission spectra of the C-dots (A). The excitation-dependent emission of the C-dots (B). The pH-dependent emission of the C-dots (C). The fluorescence decay of the C-dots (D). Insets in A and C are the photographs of pure C-dots under 365 nm UV irradiation, plot of intensity vs pH, respectively.
The synchronous responses of fluorescence decay (A) and RLS enhancement (B) to glucose.

RLS (A) and fluorescence (B) spectra of glucose titrating C-dots aqueous solution. The selectivity characterization for glucose in fluorescence (C) and RLS (D) of C-dots aqueous solution. The (IF)0 and IF in C are fluorescent intensities of the C-dots before and after adding the corresponding substances, respectively. (Deta IRLS) in D is the subtraction of RLS intensity of the C-dots after and before adding the corresponding substances, and the subscript glucose in D represents the above-mentioned value of glucose.

TEM image of C-dots with glucose (A). Effects of pH on the detection of glucose (B). F0 and F are fluorescence intensities of the C-dots in the absence and presence of glucose, respectively. Glucose concentration is 2500 μM. Effects of incubation time on the fluorescence intensities of the C-dot aqueous solution (C).

The RLS (A) and subtraction of instant (i) and original (0) intensity of RLS (B), and fluorescence (C) spectra of glucose titrating C-dots aqueous solution. The corresponding plots of [(IRLS)i − (IRLS)0],455/(IF)i,420 versus glucose concentration (D). The (IRLS)i,455, (IRLS)0,455 and (IF)i,420 represent instant RLS intensity at 455 nm wavelength, original one, and instant fluorescence at 420 nm wavelength, respectively. The inset in D is the data amplification with concentration from 0 to 250 μM.

The effects of metal ions (A) and potential biological interfering substances on fluorescence of C-dots aqueous solution (B). The concentrations of metal ions were all 10 μМ. The concentrations of glucose, calf thymus DNA, BSA, glutathione, various amino acids, urea and uric acid were 2500, 0.1, 0.1, 0.1, 1.0, 10 and 10 μМ, respectively. The fluorescence (C) and RLS (D) spectra of C-dots in water and urine.
Binding energies of the different peaks in the XPS spectra of C-dots.

head.ce:title: A hybrid ratiometric probe for glucose detection based on synchronous responses to fluorescence quenching and resonance light scattering enhancement of boronic acid functionalized carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Anhui Key Laboratory of Water Pollution Control and Wastewater Reuse, and Key Laboratory of Functional Molecule Design and Interface Process, Anhui Jianzhu University, 292 Ziyun Road, Hefei, Anhui 230022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Anhui Key Laboratory of Water Pollution Control and Wastewater Reuse
and Key Laboratory of Functional Molecule Design and Interface Process

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Enzymatic assay for glucose as a traditional protocol has always been suffering from cost-expensiveness and time-consumption, and instability and denaturation of glucose oxidase. Boronate affinity chemosensors currently became the research hotspots in the field of sugar sensing. Herein a simple but effective hybrid ratiometric platform has been addressed for glucose detection based on synchronous fluorescence quenching and resonance light scattering (RLS) enhancement of boronic acid functionalized carbon dots (C-dots). The C-dots was fabricated by one step “synthesis-modification integration” strategy using 3-aminophenyl boronic acid and sodium citrate as precursors. The retained boronic acid groups on the C-dots surface can assembly with cis-diols group of glucose to form coordination compound, which resulted in the fluorescence quenching of C-dots by aggregation states. Also, the aggregation among C-dots simultaneously induced the great RLS enhancement of C-dots because of the existence of two pairs of cis-diols groups. A hybrid ratiometric chemosensor for glucose was therefore constructed. Significantly, due to the inert surface of the C-dots, this ratiometric probe can assay down to 10 μM glucose without the interference from a variety of biomolecules and metal ions in real water samples, and exhibits an excellent selectivity over other analogues. The present sensing system has been successfully applied to the assay of glucose in urine samples. It displays great promise as a practical platform for glucose detection due to its simplicity and effectiveness.

ce:section.ce:para: Glucose is the major energy source in cellular metabolism, which plays a crucial role in the natural growth of cell. Glucose deficiency or excessive intake can result in a harmful influence on cellular functions [1]. With the improvement of living standards, diabetes mellitus has increased rapidly throughout the whole world due to the excessive intake of high-sugar foods. Diagnosis and treatment of diabetes has been caused a hot discussion of the scientific community because more than 117 million people suffered from diabetes around the world, and it was forecasted that the figure will be reached to 300 million by 2025 [2]. The World Health Organization gave much more severe statistics results in 2010, which show the number of diabetic patients is estimated to almost double to 600 million in 2030 [3,4]. The mean growth rate arrived at a surprising 4.86%. The most significant goal in the treatment of diabetes is the tight control of blood glucose [5]. As a regular control of blood glucose level, glucose detection are especially important in diabetes care and is an essential part of managing this disease [6]. Also, blood glucose monitoring guided insulin injection and further improved the prognosis for diabetes patients [7,8]. Therefore, the glucose sensing has attracted considerable research interests.

Enzymatic assays for glucose, as the most commonly used technology, have greatly contributed to correct the deviations and maintain long term near-normoglycemia of blood glucose. In the light of their sensitivity, selectivity and specificity, enzyme-based attempts to sense glucose have made great improvement in recent 3 years, such as surface-enhanced Raman scattering [9,10], electrochemistry [11–20], colorimetry [21–23], chemiluminescence [24], electrocatalysis [25–27], and so on. Moreover, optical sensing approaches have attracted rising attention due to their intrinsic advantages and considerable research space [28–33]. Fluorescence sensors offer a possibility to construct non-invasive glucose sensors in the form of a smart tattoo or contact lenses to provide comfort for diabetic patients [34]. However, most of these approaches integrate some shortcomings, for example, sophisticated instrumentation, time-consuming immobilizing process, complicated operation, readable signal drift and repeating calibration. Effective immobilization of enzyme on a materials surface is the key step and of great importance for the development of glucose sensors, but it still remains tremendous challenges to avoid enzyme denaturation and form close-packed structure [35–39]. Therefore, it is significant and urgent to develop nonenzymatic methods to overcome the above inartificial defects of enzyme-based ones. Mimics is one of the effective solutions [23,40–42]. Cyclodextrin modified gold nanoparticles [40], Cu-Ag bimetallic nanoparticles on reduced graphene oxide nanosheets [23], carbon coated magnetite nanoparticles [41], and carbon quantum dots/Cu2O nanocomposites [42] all possess unpredictable catalytic activity and simultaneously exhibit mimicking activities of both glucose oxidase and hydrogen peroxidase. These novel materials and technologies not only greatly enriched the types of glucose sensors but also efficiently eliminated the instability of enzyme-based sensors. Another effective solution is the boronate affinity strategy. Potential boronic acid derivatives are good components of the nonenzymatic optical sugar recognition, which has been widely used in the design and synthesis of sensors for cis-diol-containing compounds [43]. Such applications rely on the known thermodynamic property of boronic acid to form a tight complex with a cis-diol moiety [44]. Most recently, Xia et al. [45] reported a novel, simple and effective strategy to fabricate fluorescent boronic acid modified carbon dots (C-dots) for nonenzymatic blood glucose sensing. This one-step fabrication method and blood glucose assay application holds at least following advantages: nonuse of expensive and delicate glucose oxidase can avoid tedious and uncontrollable surface modification process; low cost and simplified procedure; avoidance of cumbersome separation and purification operation during synthesis of molecule-based boronic acid dye. The above dominances open up a promising door for both fundamental research and applications.

Herein, the one-step “synthesis-modification integration” strategy was performed to synthesize highly bright carbon dots (C-dots) using sodium citrate/3-aminophenyl boronic acid (3-APBA) as precursors for nonenzymatic urine glucose sensing [45,46]. The as-prepared C-dots can be directly functionalized by boronic acid in synthesis process without any additional surface modifications for introducing binding sites, which can totally eliminate the instability brought by immobilization of enzyme. The emission redshift of C-dots with the increase of excitation wavelength was observed. The maximum emission basically kept stable at pH range from 6.0 to 8.0 while the optimal excitation wavelength was set as 340 nm. Most interestingly, the inter-C-dots aggregation driven by boronate affinity induced the synchronous fluorescence quenching and resonance light scattering (RLS) enhancement, resulting in the further increase of ratio of RLS and fluorescence. Accordingly, a facile and effective hybrid ratiometric platform with boronic acid modified C-dots for detection of glucose was constructed, giving a distinct non-linear increases in glucose concentrations from 10 to 2000 μM with 10 μM detection limit. Importantly, glucose can well be distinguished from its analogues and various potential interfering substances by the C-dots. Due to simplicity, selectivity and stability, the C-dots have great promise as a practical platform for glucose sensing.

3-Aminophenyl boronic acid (3-APBA, 98%) was the product of Alpha Aesar (Shanghai, China). D-glucose, sucrose and D-fructose were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Doubly deionized water was prepared in Elix 5 pure water system (Millipore, Milwood, MA). All other reagents were of analytical grade and commercially purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and used without as received. Dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff = 1 000) was acquired from Shanghai Green Bird Science & Technology Development Co., China.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu 1901 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The resonance light scattering (RLS) spectra were measured by synchronously scanning the excitation and emission monochromators (Δl = 0 nm) on the F-7000 spectrophotometer. The morphology and microstructure of the C-dots were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on a JEM-200CX (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) microscope operating at a 200 kV accelerating voltage. The samples for TEM were obtained by drying sample droplets from water dispersion onto a 300- mesh Cu grid coated with a lacey carbon film, and the solvent was excluded by evaporation in air. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of C-dots were recorded on a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, JEOL, JSM 7500F). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (4000 ∼ 400 cm−1) in KBr were recorded with a Thermo-6700 spectrometer (Nicolet, Madison, WI, USA). Fluorescence lifetime measurements were recorded on a Horiba Scientific Single Photo Counting Controller: FluoroHub, and 370 nm as an excitation wavelength. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed by an XPS instrument (ESCALAB 250 Xi XPS) using Al Ka X-ray source (1486.6 eV). The acidity was measured with a Sartorius PB–10 pH meter (Sartorius, Dietikon, Switzerland). The solutions containing C-dots were analyzed for ξ-potential values using dynamic light scattering (Zetasizer Nano ZS90 series, Malvern Instruments) with 633 nm laser wavelength and a measurement angle of 173° (backscatter detection) at 25 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of boronic acid functionalized C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 0.1 g of 3-APBA and 0.716 g of sodium citrate were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 9.0 using 1.0 M NaOH under stirring followed by bubbling with N2 for 1 h to remove dissolved oxygen, and then transferred to the Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 160 °C for 8 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the C-dots containing solution was centrifuged at 10 000 rpm for 30 min to remove large precipitates. For further purifying the C-dots, the resultant solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 24 h. The purified C-dots were preserved in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Collection of real urine samples and procedures for glucose sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The urine samples from healthy volunteers were collected in precleaned glass bottles. The samples were filtered through a 3 kDa ultrafiltration tube to remove large molecules and proteins, and then stored in a refrigerator for further use.

Amounts of 100 μL of 0.1 M PBS buffer solution (pH = 8.0) and 25 μL of purified C-dots were placed into 4.0 mL colorimetric tubes. The mixtures were diluted to 3.0 mL with water or treated urine, and mixed thoroughly. Different amounts of glucose were then added to record fluorescence and RLS spectra after 1 h.

The emerging C-dots have obtained tremendous attention for their enormous potentials for application due to their physicochemical properties, high photostability, biocompatibility [47–49], as well as the hands-down precursors [50]. In general, the formation of amide bond helps to form carbogenic C-dots during carbonization of precursors, and obtains C-dots with relatively high quantum yield (QY) [46,51,52]. The cheap sodium citrate is usually selected as a donor of carboxylic acid. In addition, 3-APBA as co-precursor was considered basically owing to two reasons as follows, as well as the amine condensing to be amide: 1) the benzene ring of 3-APBA is an excellent carbon source because of high carbon content; 2) the group containing boronic acid, which is one of the most commonly used sites for glucose recognition, possibly realizes one-step “synthesis-modification integration” fabrication. Accordingly, 3-APBA and sodium citrate as precursors were adopted to prepare the C-dots with functionalization of boronic acid by one-step hydrothermal carbonization “synthesis-modification integration” strategy.

With the increase of carbonization temperature, the dehydration reaction between 3-APBA and sodium citrate firstly took place, followed by forming polymer-like composites, which were successively carbonized to take shape the C-dots (see Scheme 1 A). The chemical components of C-dots were investigated by SEM images. It was found the C-dots comprise of Carbon (C), Oxygen (O), Nitrogen (N) and Boron (B) elements (see Fig. S1). The TEM image shows that C-dots are well-dispersed without apparent aggregation, and particle size ranging from 2.6 to 4.7 nm with average size of 3.1 nm in diameter (see Fig. S2A). The XRD pattern of the C-dots displays a broad peak at about 24.2°, which is possibly attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms (Fig. S2B). The absorption bands from the surface state and composition of the C-dots were studied by FT-IR. Fig. 1 A exhibits obvious absorption bands at 1190 and 1020 cm−1, which can be ascribed to bending vibration and deformation one of BOH, respectively [45]. The peak at 1080 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of CB bond. Whereas the stretching mode of BO possibly submerges in the strong peak at range from 1316 to 1430 cm−1. In addition, the broad peak around 3420 cm−1 occurs at FTIR curve because of the stretching vibrations of COH bonds. The peak at 1429 cm−1 can be identified as COO group [46]. XPS was carried out to provide convincing evidences for the surface state of C-dots (see Table 1 ). The XPS spectrum of C-dots reveals the as-prepared C-dots is composed of C, O, N and B, which well coincided with the SEM images. Their contents are 58.17%, 34.4%, 3.64% and 3.79%, respectively. The almost same content of N and B is identical to that of 3-APBA, indicating that amine and boronic acid groups integrally transfer from 3-APBA to the C-dots during carbonization. The obvious C, O, N and B peaks were detected with binding energy at around 285.3, 531.8, 399.5 and 192.5 eV, respectively (see Fig. 1B). The high-resolution C1 s analysis revealed five peaks observed with the binding energies of about 283.9, 284.5, 285.2, 288.2 and 289.0 eV are attributed to CB, CC/CC, CO, HNCO and CCO species, respectively (see Fig. 1C) [45]. The characteristic peak of CC sp2 at 284.5 eV indicates the abundant graphite structure of C-dots [46]. The existence of NCO demonstrated that condensation reaction plays a key role in the formation of polymer-like composites during carbonization. The high-resolution B1 s spectrum shown in Fig. 1D reveals a broad peak between 190 and 195 eV, which can be disintegrated into two peaks. One is the BC bond signal occurring at 192.2 eV. The other is the peak at 192.7 eV, which is attributed to the BO bond. The above results confirm boronic acid groups retained on the surface of C-dots during process from precursors to final carbonization products at high temperature. Large amount of boronic acid, and a little carboxylic acid and hydroxyl groups anchoring on the surface of the C-dots suggested that C-dots are negatively charged. The ζ-potential of the C-dots was measured as −20.3 mV, demonstrating the above supposition (see Fig. S3).

The absorptions of C-dots and its precursors were investigated in detail here. As shown in Figs. 1A and S4A, both C-dots and their precursors (3-APBA and sodium citrate) have a conspicuous absorption band. The peak absorptions of C-dots and 3-APBA center at 280 and 295 nm, respectively, which are attributed to π-π* transition of carbon [53]. Another absorption peak around 330 nm of UV–vis spectrum of C-dots is assigned to the transition of n-π* for CO [54]. Additionally, a shoulder absorption ranging from 300 to 450 nm was found at C-dots curve, indicating the formation of C-dots. Meanwhile, a broad absorption with a center at 285 nm was observed at sodium citrate curve. Fig. 2 B exhibits the excitation-dependent emission of C-dots, with the excitation wavelengths being increased from 320 to 450 nm with an interval of 10 nm, the corresponding emission wavelengths gradually red-shifted from 415 to 540 nm (see Fig. S4B). Meanwhile, the emissions firstly enhanced with excitation wavelength red-shift, and then gradually decreased featuring 335 nm and 425 nm as maximum excitation and emission wavelengths, respectively (see Fig. 2A). Different from 3-APBA precursor with no emission at visible region, the C-dots takes on brightly blue light under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp (see Inset in Fig. 2A), verifying the new species was born via carbonization of precursors. Their fluorescence QY was calculated as ∼17% by the slope method using quinine sulfate as reference (0.1 M H2SO4 as solvent, QY = 0.54) [50], suggesting the tremendous potential of C-dots as a chemosensor.

The pH-dependent emission behavior is one of the most special properties of C-dots. Also, the effect of pH on the peak emission (425 nm) was studied at the present work. It can be seen from Fig. 2C, with pH being increased from 2.0 to 12.0, the emission enhanced firstly to maximum platform at pH 6.0 ∼ 8.0, then gradually decreased (see Inset in Fig. 2C), which possibly caused by the presence of many boronic acid and a little COO and OH group in the C-dots [46]. Moreover, the emission of the C-dots displays a redshift at strong acid condition. The fluorescence lifetime of the C-dots was detected as 6.77 ns (Fig. 2D), which is a typical fluorescence decay level, although the further study must be required to understand the exact emissive mechanism of C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synchronous responses of fluorescence quenching and RLS enhancement of C-dots for glucose
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, high enough emission efficiency makes the C-dots comparable to some previous reports [55] and competent for sensing applications. In view of the structural characteristic of C-dots functionalized by boronic acid and in order to better understand the potential of the C-dots, the effects of glucose on fluorescence and RLS spectra of C-dots were investigated at pH ∼8.0. The results indicate that fluorescence severely reduced by ∼74% with glucose being injected into C-dots aqueous solution to 2.5 mM (see Fig. 3 A). Meanwhile, the swift enhancement in RLS intensity over 1600 unit (a.u.) at 445 nm was observed at a synchronous test procedure (see Fig. 3B), implying the boronic acid functionalized C-dots have excellent synchronous responses of fluorescence decay and RLS enhancement to glucose. Also, the results suggest there should be a potential relationship between glucose concentration and dual-response signals that can be adopted as a hybrid probe for quantitative assay of glucose [56].

The relative sensitivities of fluorescence, RLS and hybrid signal of the boronic acid functionalized C-dots toward glucose were evaluated here to understand the advantage of the potential hybrid ratiometric probe, and the following equations were characterized as the sensitivities of the above-mentioned three signals:
(1)
S F
=
Δ
I F
Δ
C
(2)
S
R L S
=
Δ
I
R L S
Δ
C
(3)
S
H y b r i d
=
I
R L S
−
I F
Δ
C
where SF , SRLS and SHybrid are the sensitivities of three signals, respectively. ΔC is the change of glucose concentration, and ΔIF and ΔIRLS are the corresponding signal changes of fluorescence and RLS, respectively. IRLS and IF are the intensity of fluorescence and RLS, respectively, at a certain glucose concentration, and (IRLS − IF ), the subtraction of both. Given the change of glucose concentration was from 0 to 2.5 mM, let’s intuitively learn the sensitivity difference of three signals. It was found that the sequence of sensitivity from small to large is SF , SRLS and SHybrid in turn Y-axis at the range of total concentration through the amplified (see Fig. S5). Significantly, the SHybrid value is far larger than SF and SRLS ones, suggesting the sensing efficiency of the former was much higher than that of the latter two.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the boronic acid functionalized C-dots chemosensor for glucose
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the selectivity properties of the boronic acid functionalized C-dots for glucose over other analogues, such as maltose, lactose, fructose and sucrose, the effects of these sugar compounds on the fluorescence and RLS spectra of the C-dots aqueous solution were simultaneously investigated. With the increase of glucose concentration in the C-dots aqueous solution, the RLS gradually enhanced (see Fig. 4 A), whereas the fluorescence continually quenched (see Fig. 4B). The same experiments were also performed between the C-dots and other analogues. Although the concentrations of the glucose analogues were up to 2 mM, the changes of RLS (see Fig. S6A) and fluorescence (see Fig. S6B) were not obvious. The slight either enhancement or decay of fluorescence for glucose analogues was observed. The RLS, totally slight enhancement. The changes of fluorescence and RLS at 2000 μM sugar concentration were therefore adopted to characterize as the selectivity of C-dots. The results shown in Fig. 4C indicate the fluorescence quenching effects of these four sugars are negligible. Also, the RLS measurements gave the same result (see Fig. 4D). Both above demonstrated that the C-dots are well-suited for recognizing glucose from other saccharides. Such good specificity and resulting recognition principle were dependent on the stereostructure properties of glucose. As a result of other four analogues with no additional cis conformational diol unit, they cannot crosslink with the C-dots to form assemble and result in the corresponding fluorescent quenching and RLS enhancement [57–59], which will be elucidated in detail in Section 3.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Boron affinity interaction between C-dots and glucose, and influence factors
ce:section.ce:para: Boronic acids can reversibly coordinate with cis-diols to form stable boronate compounds. The tight but invertible recognition of diols gives rapid and sensitive response to glucose. The present boronic acid functionalized C-dots may be a potential candidate for glucose sensing. It is conceivable that if the aggregation of inter-dots happened among C-dots induced by coordination of boronic acid with dual cis-diols of glucose, the C-dots should therefore interlink together without a single dot state at the horizon of the TEM (see Scheme 1B). The TEM image shown in Fig. 5 A confirms the above inference. The fluorescence lifetime of the C-dots was detected as 6.52 ns after the introduction of glucose, which is approximately equal to that in the absence of glucose, also demonstrating the aggregation of C-dots (see Fig. S7). On the contrary, due to the mono-cis-diols structure of other four glucose analogues, though the sugar also anchored on the surface of the C-dots through the same driven force, lack of another pair of cis-diols resulted in the incapability of affinity of other C-dots. As a result, the C-dots appeared alone at the horizon (see Fig. S8). This result, together with the enhanced RLS, irrefutably demonstrates that the C-dots assemble was caused by glucose complexing instead of drying on grid. In consideration of the formation of C-dots assembly, on the one hand, the fluorescence of the C-dots is well-quenched through the aggregation states [60]. On the other hand, the non-homogeneous phase size effect caused by coordination of C-dots with glucose leads to C-dots forming much larger assemblies, the swift enhancement of RLS was then observed [61,62]. In comparison to the reported sensing motifs of glucose-induced nanoparticles aggregation [57,62], the present probe was prepared through one-step “synthesis- modification integration” strategy proposed by Xia et al. [45], which features no any post-modification procedure.

Some factors influencing boron affinity interaction between C-dots and glucose, such as interaction time and pH, were further studied to optimize the experimental conditions for better sensing performances. As shown in Fig. 5B, the quenching efficiencies of glucose seem noticeable at pH 8.0. Whereas the pH was either greater than 8.0 or lower than 7.5, the quenching efficiencies both sharply reduced. Moreover, because the formation of boronate complexes is a release proton reaction [63], a basic condition is much more favorable to form C-dots assemblies. The negative ξ-potential of C-dots possibly results from the B(OH)2, OH and COOH groups anchoring on the surface of C-dots (see Fig. S3). The pH value can modulate the electrostatic interaction among C-dots. However, when solution pH was tuned from 7.0 to 8.0, the ξ-potential values of the C-dots only slightly increased from −22.5 to −20.3 mV (see Fig. S9). This result indicates that the electrostatic repulsion keeps relatively stable at pH ranging from 7.0 to 8.0, and helps to the assembly of the C-dots at relatively higher pH value. Considering the balance between the above two effects, it was appropriate that pH 8.0 was adopted for glucose assay. Also, the quenching efficiencies are affected by the incubation time. At different glucose concentrations and within 20 min, the coordination reactions near to accomplish, but the real reaction balance comes true after 1 h (see Fig. 5C). Accordingly, all experiments were performed in aqueous solution buffered with PBS at pH 8.0 and 1 h incubation time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synchronous RS enhancement but fluorescence quenching-based hybrid ratiometric probe
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized boronic acid functionalized C-dots possess the properties of synchronous RS enhancement and fluorescence quenching in the presence of glucose, implying the C-dots have a great potential as a hybrid ratiometric chemosensor. To explore potential applications of the C-dots, the effects of glucose on the fluorescence and RLS of C-dots were concurrently investigated. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the RLS intensity gradually enhanced with the incessant addition of glucose. Due to the nanometer-sized C-dots, a fairly strong original RLS intensity was observed. In order to eliminate the effects of original RLS intensity on spectral resolution, the new RLS titration spectra were obtained through the subtraction of instant RLS intensity and original one (see Fig. 6B). Inversely, the synchronous fluorescence continually reduced (see Fig. 6C). A ratiometric plotting of the intensity changes, that is {[(IRLS)i − (IRLS)0],455/(IF)i,420}, at two different wavelengths (455 nm for RLS and 420 nm for fluorescence) with the glucose concentration shown in see Fig. 6D yielded a more sensitive response. This hybrid ratiometric plotting exhibited a distinct non-linear increases in glucose concentrations from 10 to 2000 μM. The non-linear regression equations were Y = 19.40 × [1–exp (-1.07 × 10−4C)] (where C is the concentration of glucose in μM, and Y represents the ratio of above-mentioned parameter {[(IRLS)i − (IRLS)0],455/(IF)i,420}) [64]. The correlation coefficients were 0.9966. When the added glucose concentration was 10 μM, the quenching ratio for fluorescence and increase ratio for RLS were 5.76%, indicating this probe can detect at least down to 10 μM of glucose in aqueous solution [46,65]. The synthesized C-dots can therefore be utilized as a hybrid ratiometric probe featuring high sensitivity for glucose detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of C-dots for ratiometric assay of glucose in urine
ce:section.ce:para: The blood glucose determination is the most commonly used technology, which needs frequent blood sampling and hence blood drawing [66]. This approach is an inconvenience and the need compliance by patients. Urine glucose detection as a non-invasive technique also gives guidance for effective diagnosis and management of diabetes [1,6]. In this regard, the proposed method will be intensively focused on the potential application in urine glucose sensing. The existence of COO and OH groups on the C-dots makes the coordination of C-dots with metal ions possible, which probably leads to the fluorescence quenching. So, the effects of metal ions, and potential interfering substances co-existing in urine, namely substrate effect, including various amino acids, glutathione, urea and uric acid, on glucose detection were considered. As shown in Fig. 7 A, the quenching or enhancement ratio was not more than 5%, indicating the metal ions have little response to fluorescence of C-dots aqueous solution. The non-response causes were also rationally inferred as follows: the above-mentioned O content of the C-dots is about ∼34%. The forms containing O which generally do not take part in coordinating with metal ions, such as CO, COC, NHCO, BO and CO groups, as well as OH, were deducted. The form COO− is factually little. This cause, together with the dilution of COO− groups into the “sea” of B(OH)2 ones (see Scheme 1A), resulted in the non-response of metal ions to fluorescence of C-dots. Also, the potential interfering substances co-existing in urine exerted negligible effects (see Fig. 7B). The fluorescence quenching or enhancement ratio is both not more than 6%. The cause lies in the crosslinking effect of glucose through the specific reaction of two pairs of cis-diols with boronic acid leads to the fluorescence quenching of C-dots. Due to lack of such special structure, the co-existing substances cannot lead to the aggregation of the C-dots, and corresponding fluorescence quenching and RLS enhancement. At the identical experimental conditions, the fluorescence and RLS spectra were simultaneously measured in water and pre-treated urine. As shown in Fig. 7C and D, no obvious differences were found at spectral curves, demonstrating this hybrid ratiometric chemosensor has the strong potential for urine glucose sensing. Also, the fluorescence and RLS backgrounds of urine have no obvious effect on glucose detection (see Fig.S10A and B).

The C-dots display a high fluorescence brightness, and the boronic acid groups at the surface of C-dots have a high affinity to glucose. To further investigate the potential practical applications of this hybrid ratiometric method, the glucose sensing in urine samples from healthy volunteers was carried out. The C-dots and buffer solution were added into the 3 mL pre-treated urine samples and incubated for 1 h, and then the fluorescence and RLS intensities were simultaneously measured. As shown in Table 2 , the glucose contents in urine samples of healthy volunteers obtained by the proposed method were not detectable. After the addition of various glucose concentrations, the measured values are well in agreement with the spiked glucose amounts with recoveries from 98.4% to 104.5%, indicating the practicability of the developed sensing platform.

In summary, 3-APBA and sodium citrate as the precursors were co-carbonized at 160 °C for 8 h to prepare boronic acid functionalized C-dots with high relatively QY. Boronic acid groups at the surface of the C-dots can complex with dual cis-diols of glucose to form large assemblies, resulting in the synchronous fluorescence quenching by aggregation states and RLS enhancement by non-homogeneous phase size effect. A hybrid ratiometric chemosensor was therefore developed for facile and selective sensing of glucose in urine samples at pH 8.0. More notably, the present glucose probe was obtained by a one-step method, and any additional surface modifications were needless. The C-dots-based sensing platform exhibits high sensitivity, wide linear response range and low cost, being promising as an efficient platform for quantitative glucose monitoring, and enriching the applications of nonenzymatic glucose sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of China (51378017, 21506002), and Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (1608085MB37) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.05.115.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Wen-Sheng Zou is an associate professor at Key Laboratory of Functional Molecule Design and Interface Process, Anhui Jianzhu University. He got a PhD from Nanjing University in 2011. His current research interests focus on the design and synthesis of organic fluorescent/phosphorescent molecules, and fluorescent carbon nanomaterials for biological species detection.

Cui-Hong Ye received her bachelor degree in 2015 from Western Anhui University. She is currently a master’s graduate student at Department of Municipal Engineering, Anhui Jianzhu University. Her research interests focus on the analysis and detection of carbohydrate in tail water.

Ya-Qin Wang is currently a professor at school of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Anhui Jianzhu University. She received his master degree in chemical technology from Anhui University of Science and Technology. And She achieved a PhD of polymeric chemistry and physics from University of Science and Technology of China in 2015. Her research interests focus on the membrane fabrication and application.

Wei-Hua Li is currently a professor at the Key Laboratory of Water Pollution Control and Wastewater Reuse in Anhui province. He received his master degree (2004) in optics from Anhui institute of optics and fine mechanics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. And he achieved a PhD of environmental science from University of Science and Technology of China in 2008. His current research mainly focused on characterizing the wastewater treatment process using spectral method.

Xian-Huai Huang is currently a professor at the Key Laboratory of Water Pollution Control and Wastewater Reuse in Anhui province. He received his master degree (1989) in environmental engineering from Harbin University of Civil Engineering and Architecture, Chinese Academy of Sciences. And he achieved a PhD of material science and technology from University of Science and Technology of China in 2005. His current research mainly focused on material science research in wastewater treatment process.
